children.name: Salmo
children.description: Atlantic salmon and brown trout — the two most culturally significant fish in northern European freshwaters. The Atlantic salmon's life cycle is one of nature's great dramas: born in cold, fast-flowing rivers, the juveniles smolt and migrate to sea, spending 1–4 years feeding in the open North Atlantic before returning with extraordinary precision to the exact river and often the exact stretch where they were born. This natal homing uses a combination of magnetic field sensing and smell — they can detect infinitesimally small concentrations of the specific mineral and biological signature of their home stream. Swedish salmon rivers like Mörrumsån and Lagan are among the most celebrated sport fishing destinations in Europe.

children.name: Salmo salar
children.description: Once incredibly abundant in Swedish rivers including the Göta älv and Dal älv; virtually eliminated from many by hydropower dams in the 20th century. Wild populations are now conservation priorities. Farmed salmon is now one of Sweden's most consumed foods. Males develop a hooked jaw (kype) during breeding season.

children.name: Salmo trutta
children.description: The most widespread salmonid in Europe and a fish of extraordinary ecological flexibility — some populations are purely freshwater residents (öring), others migrate to sea (havsöring). Sea trout of the Baltic are larger, stronger, and darker-fleshed than their freshwater forms. Extensively introduced worldwide and considered invasive in parts of the southern hemisphere.

children.name: Salmo marmoratus
children.description: A distinctive trout of the Adriatic basin in Slovenia and Italy, named for its marbled vermiculated pattern. One of Europe's largest trout, historically exceeding 20 kg; threatened by hybridisation with introduced brown trout.

children.name: Salmo ferox
children.description: A large, long-lived piscivorous ecomorph of brown trout found in deep oligotrophic lakes of the British Isles and Scandinavia, feeding on Arctic char. Can exceed 10 kg and live over 20 years.

children.name: Salmo obtusirostris
children.description: An archaic relict salmonid endemic to a few Adriatic-basin rivers of the Balkans, with a distinctive soft, downturned mouth. Considered a living fossil; IUCN Endangered due to hybridisation and habitat loss.

children.name: Salmo carpio
children.description: A trout endemic to Italy's Lake Garda, spawning at great depth. Critically Endangered due to overfishing and competition with introduced species; one of Europe's most threatened salmonids.

children.name: Salmo akairos
children.description: A brown trout relative endemic to cold spring-fed streams of the Epirus region in northwestern Greece. It inhabits fast-flowing, well-oxygenated karstic spring waters and is considered a relict species isolated since the Pleistocene glaciations.

children.name: Salmo balcanicus
children.description: A small-bodied trout endemic to streams of the Macedonian region of the Balkans, adapted to fast-flowing mountain torrents. It is recognised by its distinctive spotting pattern and smaller size compared to other Balkan trouts; threatened by habitat degradation.

children.name: Salmo cettii
children.description: A Mediterranean trout native to Corsica, Sardinia and parts of southern Italy, where it inhabits small, cool streams. It is threatened by hybridisation with introduced brown trout and habitat degradation from water abstraction.

children.name: Salmo dentex
children.description: A large predatory trout of the Adriatic basin in Bosnia, Croatia and Montenegro, distinguished by its prominent teeth adapted for piscivory. It is a top predator in karstic rivers and has declined due to dam construction and overfishing.

children.name: Salmo fibreni
children.description: A deep-water trout endemic to Lake Posta Fibreno in central Italy, where it spawns exclusively in submerged karstic springs. It was described as a distinct species relatively recently and is considered critically endangered given its tiny single-lake range.

children.name: Salmo ischchan
children.description: An endemic trout complex of Armenia's Lake Sevan, comprising four distinct morphs adapted to different spawning depths and timings — summer, winter, and two spring-spawning forms. The population collapsed after artificial water level drawdown and the introduction of whitefish; some morphs are now extinct.

children.name: Salmo labrax
children.description: An anadromous salmon of the Black Sea and Azov Sea basins, closely related to the Atlantic salmon but adapted to warmer, less saline waters. It spawns in rivers from Bulgaria to Russia and is threatened by overfishing and barriers to migration.

children.name: Salmo letnica
children.description: A celebrated trout endemic to North Macedonia's Lake Ohrid, one of Europe's oldest and deepest lakes, formed over 4 million years ago. It is the basis of a renowned local fishery and is distinguished by its silvery body with orange-tinted fins and delicate flavour.

children.name: Salmo ohridanus
children.description: A small, endemic trout of Lake Ohrid distinct from Salmo letnica in morphology and ecology, feeding on plankton and smaller fish. Known locally as belvica, it is an important commercial species in North Macedonia and is considered Near Threatened.

children.name: Salmo platycephalus
children.description: A critically endangered trout endemic to a single river drainage in the Dinaric Alps of Bosnia and Herzegovina. It is named for its unusually flattened head and is threatened by habitat loss, water extraction and hybridisation with introduced brown trout.

children.name: Salmo peristericus
children.description: A trout endemic to the Prespa Lakes shared by Greece, Albania and North Macedonia, one of the oldest lake systems in Europe. It spawns in the lakes' tributary streams and is threatened by water abstraction for irrigation and introduced species.

children.name: Salvelinus
children.description: Char — cold-adapted salmonids of glacial lakes and Arctic rivers, often the only fish present in the deepest, coldest, most oligotrophic Scandinavian lakes. The Arctic char (röding) is Sweden's most prized freshwater fish by those who know it — its flesh is salmon-pink and richly flavoured from the cold, clear waters it inhabits. Char populations have been isolated in deep Swedish lakes since the last Ice Age 10,000 years ago and have diverged into dozens of distinct local forms ('ecomorphs') that differ in body shape, feeding habits, and timing — a living laboratory of rapid evolution. Sweden has more distinct Arctic char populations than any other country.

children.name: Salvelinus alpinus
children.description: Sweden's most ecologically diverse freshwater fish, existing in hundreds of genetically and morphologically distinct lake populations. The deepest-dwelling form in Sweden's Lake Vättern reaches 100 m. Intensely red-orange on the belly during spawning — one of the most beautiful freshwater fish in the world.

children.name: Salvelinus fontinalis
children.description: Native to eastern North America; widely introduced to European mountain streams as a sport fish. Its stunning colouration — crimson spots ringed with blue on an olive-green body — makes it arguably the most beautiful freshwater fish in the world. Hybridises with brown trout to produce 'tiger trout'.

children.name: Salvelinus namaycush
children.description: The largest char, native to cold deep lakes of northern North America and reaching over 30 kg. A slow-growing, long-lived top predator; populations were devastated in the Great Lakes by sea lamprey and overfishing before restoration efforts.

children.name: Salvelinus umbla
children.description: A deep-lake char of the Alpine lakes of central Europe, sometimes treated as a form of Arctic char. Prized in Alpine cuisine; populations are sensitive to eutrophication and warming.

children.name: Salvelinus leucomaenis
children.description: A char of Japan, the Russian Far East and Korea, known in Japanese as iwana. Inhabits cold mountain streams and exists in both resident and sea-run forms.

children.name: Salvelinus malma
children.description: A widespread char of the North Pacific rim named after a brightly dressed Dickens character, with vivid spotting. Anadromous and resident forms occur; ecologically important in salmon-bearing rivers where it feeds on eggs.

children.name: Salvelinus confluentus
children.description: A large, predatory char of the Pacific Northwest and western Canada, requiring cold, clean rivers connected to deep lakes to complete its lifecycle. Listed as Vulnerable in the US and designated a species of special concern due to habitat fragmentation from dams and warming waters.

children.name: Salvelinus agassizii
children.description: A char endemic to a few lakes in New Hampshire and Massachusetts, declared extinct in 1939 due to eutrophication and competition from introduced species. Named after the Swiss-American naturalist Louis Agassiz.

children.name: Salvelinus grayi
children.description: An extinct char formerly endemic to Lough Melvin in Ireland, last recorded in the late 19th century. Named after the zoologist John Edward Gray; its extinction is attributed to eutrophication and overfishing.

children.name: Salvelinus neiva
children.description: A char endemic to Lake Neiva and other lakes of the Ural Mountains in European Russia. It is a glacial relict species, closely related to Arctic char but isolated in deep, cold Ural lakes since the last ice age.

children.name: Thymallus
children.description: Grayling — elegant, sail-finned salmonids of fast-flowing, cold, clear rivers across the Northern Hemisphere. The European grayling (harr) is distinguished by its enormous, colourful dorsal fin — used in courtship displays — and its characteristic thyme-like smell when fresh (the genus name means 'smelling of thyme'). Grayling are regarded by many as the most beautiful freshwater fish in Europe and are fastidiously selective fly-fishing targets. They are stream quality indicators: grayling require cold, clean, well-oxygenated water and disappear from rivers that warm or accumulate silt. Their presence signals a high-quality watercourse.

children.name: Thymallus thymallus
children.description: Sweden's most elegant stream fish. Found in cold, fast rivers from southern Sweden to Lapland. Its large, sail-like dorsal fin is used in courtship — males erect it to display iridescent purple and green colouration. An excellent indicator of river health.

children.name: Thymallus arcticus
children.description: The grayling of Siberia and northern North America. Montana's state fish — extirpated from the lower 48 states by early 20th century but persisting in Montana and Alaska. Will rise to a dry fly with spectacular leaps.

children.name: Thymallus baicalensis
children.description: A grayling endemic to Lake Baikal and its tributary rivers in Siberia, distinguished by its particularly large and colourful dorsal fin. It is adapted to the cold, oxygen-rich waters of the world's deepest and most voluminous freshwater lake.

children.name: Thymallus grubii
children.description: A grayling of the Amur River basin in eastern Russia and adjacent China, with a distribution that also extends into Mongolia. It is a popular sport fish in the region and is morphologically variable across its range.

children.name: Thymallus brevirostris
children.description: A grayling native to lakes and rivers of Mongolia and the Tyva Republic of Russia, named for its relatively short snout. It inhabits cold, high-altitude waters and is an important species in the region's subsistence and sport fisheries.

children.name: Oncorhynchus
children.description: Pacific salmon and rainbow trout — the most ecologically important fish of the Pacific Rim. Pacific salmon are semelparous: after a single spawning migration, every individual dies. The decaying carcasses of millions of salmon returning to Pacific rivers fertilise the surrounding forest with marine-derived nitrogen and phosphorus — nutrients carried hundreds of kilometres inland by bears, eagles, and ravens. This marine-to-terrestrial nutrient subsidy is so significant that scientists can trace it in the growth rings of trees kilometres from any river. Rainbow trout (regnbåge) is widely farmed and has been introduced to rivers across Sweden and Europe.

children.name: Oncorhynchus mykiss
children.description: The world's most widely farmed and sport-fished salmonid. Introduced to Sweden and dozens of other countries for aquaculture and sport fishing; can hybridise with and displace native brown trout. Sea-run form ('steelhead') is considered a different fish in ecology and behaviour.

children.name: Oncorhynchus nerka
children.description: The only Pacific salmon that obligately rears in a freshwater lake before going to sea. Spawning males turn vivid red with green heads — the most dramatically coloured of all Pacific salmon. Some runs in Alaska still number in the tens of millions.

children.name: Oncorhynchus tshawytscha
children.description: The largest Pacific salmon, reaching 61 kg. Called 'king salmon' in Alaska. Its fat-rich flesh is the basis of much of the Pacific Northwest's fishing industry. Introduced to New Zealand where it runs in South Island rivers — far from its native range.

children.name: Oncorhynchus kisutch
children.description: A medium-sized Pacific salmon prized by anglers for its acrobatic fights. Spawning males turn bright red on the flanks; juveniles rear in streams for a year before going to sea. Several southern populations are threatened.

children.name: Oncorhynchus gorbuscha
children.description: The smallest and most abundant Pacific salmon, with a strict two-year life cycle. Spawning males develop a pronounced hump. Has colonised rivers in northern Europe after introductions to Russia, becoming invasive in Scandinavia.

children.name: Oncorhynchus keta
children.description: A Pacific salmon with the widest natural range, spawning in lower river reaches. Spawning fish develop striking purple and green tiger-striped flanks. A major commercial species, especially for its roe (ikura).

children.name: Oncorhynchus clarkii
children.description: A western North American trout named for the red-orange slash marks under the jaw, with many subspecies adapted to distinct watersheds. Several subspecies are threatened by hybridisation and habitat loss.

children.name: Oncorhynchus apache
children.description: A golden-yellow trout endemic to the White Mountains of Arizona and Arizona's state fish. Once near extinction, it has recovered through conservation on tribal lands and is a US recovery success story.

children.name: Oncorhynchus aguabonita
children.description: A brilliantly coloured trout native to icy headwater streams of California's Sierra Nevada and the California state freshwater fish. Renowned for its golden-yellow flanks and red lateral stripe; threatened by hybridisation.

children.name: Oncorhynchus chrysogaster
children.description: A brilliantly coloured golden trout endemic to high-elevation streams of the Sierra Madre Occidental in northwestern Mexico. It is the southernmost native trout species in North America and is threatened by habitat loss and introduced trout species.

children.name: Oncorhynchus gilae
children.description: A golden-olive trout native to the Gila River basin of Arizona and New Mexico, one of the rarest trout species in North America. It was among the first fish listed under the US Endangered Species Act and has recovered from near-extinction through intensive reintroduction efforts.

children.name: Oncorhynchus masou
children.description: The only Pacific salmon native exclusively to Asia, found in rivers of Japan, the Korean Peninsula and the Russian Far East. It has several subspecies or morphs, including the anadromous form and the landlocked yamame, and is highly valued in Japanese cuisine and sport fishing.

children.name: Oncorhynchus rhodurus
children.description: A large, lake-dwelling salmonid endemic to Japan's Lake Biwa, the country's largest and oldest lake. Sometimes treated as a subspecies of masu salmon, it is distinguished by its reddish coloration and is a target of commercial and recreational fisheries.

children.name: Oncorhynchus formosanus
children.description: Taiwan's only native salmonid, endemic to a single stream high in the Hsuehshan Mountains. Critically Endangered with a population of only a few hundred individuals, it is a relict isolated since the last Ice Age and is the southernmost natural salmonid population in the world.

children.name: Oncorhynchus kawamurae
children.description: A deepwater landlocked salmon endemic to Japan's Lake Tazawa, declared extinct in 1940 after acidification from a hydroelectric project. Remarkably rediscovered in 2010 in Lake Saiko, where eggs had been translocated decades earlier; the first Japanese fish saved from extinction by a reintroduced population.

children.name: Coregonus
children.description: Whitefishes and ciscoes — silvery, plankton- and bottom-feeding salmonids of cold northern lakes and rivers. Coregonus is one of the most taxonomically complex fish genera, with numerous lake-endemic forms that have radiated rapidly since the last Ice Age. Many forms are commercially important and several have gone extinct through eutrophication.

children.name: Coregonus lavaretus
children.description: A widespread and morphologically variable whitefish (sik) of northern European lakes and rivers, important in Scandinavian commercial and subsistence fisheries. Its roe is a prized delicacy.

children.name: Coregonus albula
children.description: A small, slender, planktivorous whitefish whose roe (löjrom) from the Gulf of Bothnia is a celebrated Swedish delicacy with protected designation. Forms huge schools in cold northern lakes.

children.name: Coregonus clupeaformis
children.description: A commercially vital deepwater whitefish of North American lakes and the backbone of Great Lakes fisheries. Bottom-feeds on invertebrates; populations are affected by invasive mussels altering food webs.

children.name: Coregonus artedi
children.description: The most abundant and widespread cisco of the Great Lakes and other large North American lakes, also known as lake herring. It is a planktivorous fish that was once the basis of a massive commercial fishery; populations have declined due to eutrophication and invasive species.

children.name: Coregonus autumnalis
children.description: A migratory whitefish of Arctic river systems across Siberia, Alaska and northern Canada, named for its autumn spawning run. It is an important subsistence fish for Indigenous communities along the Arctic coast and can travel hundreds of kilometres upstream.

children.name: Coregonus hoyi
children.description: A deepwater cisco of the Great Lakes, named for the tendency of its inflated swim bladder to cause bloating when brought to the surface. It was commercially important in the early 20th century but declined sharply due to overfishing and predation by sea lamprey and alewife.

children.name: Coregonus huntsmani
children.description: A critically endangered whitefish endemic to only three lakes in Nova Scotia's Petite Riviere watershed, Canada. It is the only anadromous whitefish in eastern North America and requires access between freshwater lakes and the ocean to complete its lifecycle.

children.name: Coregonus kiyi
children.description: A small, deep-water cisco of the Great Lakes, once abundant in lakes Superior, Michigan, Huron, and Ontario but now restricted to Lake Superior due to predation by introduced sea lamprey and rainbow smelt. It inhabits cold, oxygen-rich depths of 100–400 m and feeds on copepods and other zooplankton. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Coregonus nasus
children.description: A large, distinctive whitefish of Arctic and subarctic rivers and lakes from Alaska and Canada east to Siberia, named for its broad, overhanging snout. It is a bottom-feeder on molluscs, crustaceans, and insects in clear, cold river systems and is an important subsistence food for Indigenous peoples throughout its range.

children.name: Coregonus peled
children.description: A large, commercially important whitefish of Siberian river systems from the Pechora east to the Lena, and introduced across much of European Russia for aquaculture. It is a filter-feeder on zooplankton in large lakes and slow-flowing rivers, forming large shoals in open water. An economically significant species in Russian freshwater fisheries.

children.name: Coregonus sardinella
children.description: A slender, herring-like cisco of Arctic and subarctic lakes and rivers from Alaska and northern Canada to Siberia. It is highly migratory, moving between rivers and coastal marine waters, and forms dense schools that are heavily exploited by seabirds, marine mammals, and subsistence fishers during its annual migrations.

children.name: Hucho
children.description: Taimens and huchen — enormous, elongate, predatory salmonids of Eurasian rivers, among the largest freshwater fish of the salmon family. Apex river predators that hunt fish, amphibians and even small mammals; all species are threatened by damming, overfishing and habitat degradation.

children.name: Hucho hucho
children.description: A large predatory salmonid endemic to the Danube basin, reaching 1.5 m and over 30 kg. IUCN Endangered, threatened by dams and pollution; the focus of major reintroduction efforts in central Europe.

children.name: Hucho taimen
children.description: The world's largest salmonid, reaching over 2 m and 100 kg, inhabiting cold rivers across Siberia and Mongolia. A long-lived apex predator and renowned catch-and-release sport fish; IUCN Vulnerable due to overfishing.

children.name: Prosopium
children.description: Round whitefishes — distinctive Coregoninae with a round, narrow, nearly cylindrical body cross-section (the genus name means 'roundish') and a single fleshy flap between the nostrils. They are cold-water specialists of northern lakes and rivers across North America and Siberia, often inhabiting shallower, faster water than their Coregonus relatives. Several species are endemic to single lake systems and are exceptionally vulnerable to introduced predators.

children.name: Prosopium cylindraceum
children.description: The most widespread species of round whitefish, found across northern North America and Siberia in cold lakes and rivers. It is distinguished by its slender, cylindrical body and small, delicate mouth adapted for bottom-feeding on small invertebrates.

children.name: Prosopium coulterii
children.description: The smallest member of the whitefish subfamily, rarely exceeding 15 cm, native to cold lakes in northwestern North America. A glacial relict species found in deep, oligotrophic lakes where it feeds on zooplankton and small benthic invertebrates.

children.name: Prosopium gemmifer
children.description: A cisco-like round whitefish endemic to Bear Lake on the Utah-Idaho border, one of only two lakes where this species occurs. It is a pelagic planktivore with a relatively short lifespan, spawning in shallow water in late winter.

children.name: Prosopium spilonotus
children.description: A round whitefish also endemic to Bear Lake on the Utah-Idaho border, coexisting with the Bonneville cisco but growing larger and living longer. Its common name refers to the ancient Lake Bonneville basin, a Pleistocene remnant that isolated this species.

children.name: Prosopium williamsoni
children.description: A round whitefish of cool, clear streams and lakes throughout western North America from the Rocky Mountains to the Pacific coast. It is a popular sport fish for fly anglers and an indicator species for water quality in mountain streams.

children.name: Stenodus
children.description: Inconnu, sheefish and belvica — large, predatory whitefishes with a terminal mouth and elongated body built for chasing prey in open water. The name Stenodus means 'narrow tooth', referring to their slender conical teeth that distinguish them from all other Coregoninae. They are the only piscivorous whitefishes and can exceed 20 kg, making them among the largest salmonids of Arctic and subarctic waters.

children.name: Stenodus nelma
children.description: A large, predatory whitefish of Arctic rivers from northwestern Canada to Siberia, reaching up to 20 kg — the largest member of the whitefish subfamily. Known as sheefish in Alaska and inconnu in Canada, it is a prized sport fish that makes long spawning migrations and feeds on other fish.

children.name: Stenodus leucichthys
children.description: A Caspian Sea whitefish that migrates into the Volga and Ural rivers to spawn, also known as the Caspian inconnu. Critically Endangered due to overfishing, damming and habitat loss in the Volga basin; the population has declined catastrophically since the mid-20th century.

children.name: Brachymystax
children.description: Lenoks — primitive, large-scaled salmonids of Siberian and East Asian rivers with a distinctive blunt snout and small mouth, representing the most basal surviving lineage of Salmoninae. The genus name means 'short whisker', referring to the reduced maxillary bone. They are obligate cold-water specialists restricted to pristine, well-oxygenated rivers and have been extirpated from much of their southern range by warming, damming and overfishing.

children.name: Brachymystax lenok
children.description: The more widely distributed of the two lenok species, ranging from the Ob River in western Siberia to the rivers of the Russian Far East, Mongolia and northern China. A prized sport fish growing to over 6 kg, it has declined across much of its range from overfishing and habitat degradation.

children.name: Brachymystax tumensis
children.description: A lenok species distinguished by its blunter snout and smaller maximum size, native to the Amur River basin and rivers of the Russian Far East. It overlaps in range with the sharp-snouted lenok but spawns earlier and occupies slower reaches of rivers.

children.name: Brachymystax savinovi
children.description: A recently described lenok species from the Irtysh River basin in Kazakhstan and Mongolia, representing a distinct genetic lineage within the Brachymystax complex. It is restricted to high-altitude, cold-water tributaries and is the least well-known of the three lenok species.
